coredata.dc:title: The carbon dots in toasted shrimp: Structural and biological characteristics
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Tao
Peng, Shiyi
Tu, Zongcai
Chao, Yue
Zhan, Shengnan
coredata.dc:description: The carbon dots (CDs) in toasted shrimp were extracted and their structural and biological characteristics were evaluated. The results showed that the derived CDs were monodispersed and irregularly shaped, showing great blue fluorescence. With the increasing of toasting temperature and time, CDs' size was decreased along with the CO/C–H bonds broken. The pH (2.0–10.0) had slight influence on the fluorescence intensity (FI) of CDs, but the higher contents of Na+, K+ and Mg2+, significantly increased FI value. Besides, In vitro digestion analysis showed that the CDs’ fluorescence could be quenched during stomach digestion. Fluorescence titration revealed that CDs could spontaneously (ΔG°<0) interact with hemoglobin (Hb) to form protein corona by the formation of hydrogen bonds and van der Waals force thus leading to the FI change. Moreover, CDs could cause Hb became loosened and unfolded, which might decrease the biological characteristics of Hb.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Blue fluorescence
In vitro digestion
Fluorescence titration
Hemoglobin
Protein corona

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The nuclear magnetic resonance of H1 (NMR-H1) spectra analysis
Analysis of Hb interact with CDs
Characterization of CDs from the roasted shrimp
Simulated digestion of CDs in vitro
Mechanism of CDs interacted with Hb

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Young Science and Technology Innovation Leading Talents of Ningbo ( 2023QL037 ), Young PHD Innovation Research Plan of Ningbo ( 2023J380 ), National Key R&D Program of China ( 2020YFD0900903 ), the Open Project Program of National R&D Center for Freshwater Fish Processing, Jiangxi Normal University ( NC2FP-KF-202301 ).

This work was financially supported by the Young Science and Technology Innovation Leading Talents of Ningbo (2023QL038), Young PHD Innovation Research Plan of Ningbo (2023J380), National Key R&D Program of China (2020YFD0900903), the Open Project Program of National R&D Center for Freshwater Fish Processing, Jiangxi Normal University (NC2FP-KF-202301).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images and photographs of CDs aqueous solutions under excitation with 365 nm UV light. 180–40, 200–40, 230–40, 230–20 and 230-30 meant that the CDs was extracted from the toasted shrimps by toasting temperature of 180, 200, 230 °C for 40, 20 and 30 min, respectively.
Various toasting temperature and time on the structural characteristics of CDs. FT-IR (A, B), UV–visible spectra (C) and NMR 1H (D).
Emission spectra of the CDs at excitation wavelengths progressively increased from 320 to 400 nm.
Effect of pH (A), Na+ (B), K+ (C) and Mg2+ (D) contents on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs with different concentrations of Hb. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, Hb and CDs-Hb. (C) CD spectra changes of Hb with increasing concentration of CDs. (D) α-helix, β-sheet, β-turn and random coil of the CDs-Hb system. (CDs were obtained from 230 °C for 40 min toasted shrimps).
The Stern-Volmer (A) and double logarithmic (B) plots of Hb quenching the fluorescence of the CDs at 298 K, 304 K, and 310 K (CDs were obtained from 230 °C for 40 min toasted shrimps).
Binding and thermodynamics parameters between the interaction of CDs (prepared at 230 °C, 40 min) and Hb at different temperatures.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Ksv: the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, Kq: the fluorescence quenching rate constan, Ka: the binding constant, n: the number of binding points, ΔH°: thermodynamic parameters enthalpy change, ΔS°: entropy change, ΔG°: free energy change.

head.ce:title: The carbon dots in toasted shrimp: Structural and biological characteristics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Ningbo University, Ningbo, Zhejiang Province, 315211, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Ningbo University, Ningbo, Zhejiang Province, 315211, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National R&D Center for Freshwater Fish Processing, Jiangxi Normal University, Nanchang, 330022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National R&D Center for Freshwater Fish Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National R&D Center for Freshwater Fish Processing, Jiangxi Normal University, Nanchang 330022, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The carbon dots (CDs) in toasted shrimp were extracted and their structural and biological characteristics were evaluated. The results showed that the derived CDs were monodispersed and irregularly shaped, showing great blue fluorescence. With the increasing of toasting temperature and time, CDs' size was decreased along with the CO/C–H bonds broken. The pH (2.0–10.0) had slight influence on the fluorescence intensity (FI) of CDs, but the higher contents of Na+, K+ and Mg2+, significantly increased FI value. Besides, In vitro digestion analysis showed that the CDs’ fluorescence could be quenched during stomach digestion. Fluorescence titration revealed that CDs could spontaneously (ΔG°<0) interact with hemoglobin (Hb) to form protein corona by the formation of hydrogen bonds and van der Waals force thus leading to the FI change. Moreover, CDs could cause Hb became loosened and unfolded, which might decrease the biological characteristics of Hb.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a new class of nanoscale carbon nanomaterials (<10 nm), which were initially discovered in the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes (Xu et al., 2004). CDs have the superior properties, such as low toxicity, cost-effective, great fluorescence and bio-compatibility. The above excellent functional properties entrust CDs with potential application values in bio-imaging (Jiang et al., 2014), bio-analysis (Chandra et al., 2016) and biosensors (Ye et al., 2017). With the developments of nanotechnology, similar nanoparticles were also be found during food thermal processing, eg grilled fish, coffee, hamburger, which were normally named as food-borne fluorescent CDs (Li et al., 2019).

The generated CDs was closely related to the baking temperature and time. Bi et al. (2018) explained that carbohydrates, proteins and lipids in food could spontaneously react with each other and formed heterogeneous structures through thermally induced polymerization. These heterogeneous structures gradually disintegrated through the pyrolysis procedure, contributed to the seed-mediated nucleation and contraction, leading to the formation of CDs. The higher temperature and the longer baking time were more prone to cause the breakage of carbon-carbon bonds, conducing to form lots of CDs from the fragmented biopolymer residues (Bi et al., 2018).

Barbecue foods are very favored by many people because of its unique aroma, distinctive flavor and savory taste. As a typical ingredient in barbecue, the toasted shrimp is one of the most warmly welcomed midnight snacks in China. Whereas, some organic chemicals generated during extremely high temperature were linked to the increased risk of various cancer forms (eg. pancreatic cancer, gastric cancer) (Jiao et al., 2015). During the high temperature of roasting, carbohydrates, proteins and lipids in food could spontaneously aggregate and form CDs through non-enzymatic-browning Maillard reaction (Bi et al., 2018). CDs could be absorbed into the body after consumption, furtherly interacted with proteins in blood, influencing the physiological function of proteins (Cui, Su, & Tan, 2022; Liu, Liu, Cui, & Tan, 2022). Cui et al. (2022) explained that CDs could spontaneously adsorb in vivo protein during blood transport and formed protein corona inside the human body, influencing the activities of active-protein and bio-enzyme. Song, Cao, et al. (2019) found that CDs from roasted fish could interact with digestive protease and dopamine via the static quenching mechanism. However, there is still rare information focusing on the formation and biological characteristics of CDs in roasted shrimp.

Hemoglobin (Hb) is a well-known tetrameric metalloprotein in blood, which could transport oxygen and carbon dioxide. As the major component and the only non-membrane protein of red blood cells (Chi, Zhao, You, & Wang, 2016), Hb might also interact with CDs rapidly and influence its biological function. Therefore, the effects of CDs on Hb in blood require more rigorous evaluation. Furthermore, CDs inhibit hydroxyl radical activity by reacting with it directly or via inhibiting the formation of hydroxyl radical indirectly (Li et al., 2022). However, CDs could combine with other substances during digestion and rarely act independently. Thus, it is not sufficient to evaluate the biological properties of CDs only from its structural characteristics.

Thus, this study aimed to thoroughly evaluate the basic characteristics of CDs in the toasted shrimp through morphology, fluorescence stability and in vitro analysis, and the biological properties of CDs through the interaction mechanism of CDs and Hb were also investigate. This research might help consumers better understand the potential defects of toasted shrimp and Barbecue industry through another perspective.

Frozen peeled shrimp (Solenocera crassicornis) was bought from Zhejiang Tianhe Aquatic Products Inc., ltd. (Taizhou, Zhejiang Province, China). Bovine hemoglobin was purchased from Solarbio life science (Beijing, China). Artificial saliva, gastric juice and small bowel fluid were acquired from Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). HepG2 cells were purchased from Procell Life Science & Technology Co., Ltd (Wuhan, Hubei Province, China). Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM), trypsin and phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) were obtained from Corning (Shanghai, China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was bought from Aladdin Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The other chemical reagents were of analytical grade.

The CDs was extracted according to the method of Bi et al., 2018. The shelled shrimp (500 g each group) were roasted in a Supor, K35FK602 electric heating oven (Supor, Hangzhou, Zhejiang Province, China) at 180 °C, 200 °C, 230 °C for 40 min and at 230 °C for 20 min, 30 min, 40 min, respectively. The roasted shrimp were immediately mashed and soaked in 100% ethanol, stirring continuously at room temperature for 24 h. The large agglomerates were removed by filtration. Then, the crude extraction was dried out by evaporation before being re-dissolved in ultrapure water. After being degreased by chloroform, the mixture was centrifuged with 10,000 rpm for 10 min at 4 °C. Following, the supernatant was transferred into the dialysis membrane (500 Da) and dialyzed for 48 h to remove residues at room temperature. Subsequently, the dialysis solution was collected and lyophilized. Finally, the obtained CDs powders were then gathered for further analysis. All the reagents were of analytical grade.

Brifely, CDs (1 mg/mL) soulution was dripped onto the ultra-thin carbon film, and then the sample was natural dried at room temperature. The morphology of CDs was collected using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM-H7650, Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). The acceleration voltage was 80 kV. The Image J software was used to calculate the CDs size.

The FTIR spectra of CDs were recorded using the FTIR spectrometer (FTIR-4700, JASCO Corporation, Tokyo, Japan). The samples were mixed with KBr and ground thoroughly, then the mixture was pressed into thin slices. The FTIR spectra was recorded at wavelength number of 400–4000 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs (1 mg/mL) were recorded using UV–vis spectrophotometer (TU-1810, Puxi, Beijing, China). The spectm of each sample was collected in the range of 200–700 nm with 2400 nm/min scanning rate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The nuclear magnetic resonance of H1 (NMR-H1) spectra analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs (1 mg/mL) solution was prepared using D2O. The NMR-H1 spectra of liquid CDs were obtained using NMR spectrometer (Bruker AVANCE II 400 MHz, Boston, Massachusetts, USA). The magnetic field intensity was 400 MHz with spectral width of 8012.8 Hz, relation time was 1.5 s.

A modified method based on Jiang et al., 2014 was used to determine the fluorescence stability of CDs. The Rritton-Robinsin buffer with pH (2.0–11.0) were prepared, which consisting of 0.04 mol/L H3PO4, 0.04 mol/L H3BO3 and 0.04 mol/L CH2COOH. The pH on the fluorescence stablility of CDs were evaluated. Various contents (1 mmol/L, 50 mmol/L and 100 mmol/L) of salts (NaCl, KCl and MgCl2) were used to evaluate the fluorescence stability of CDs. Briefly, 200 μL 1 mg/mL CDs solution was added to 1.8 mL Rritton-Robinsin buffer, NaCl, KCl and MgCl2 solution respectively. The fluorescence spectra of CDs was recorded using fluorescence spectrometer. The excitation wavelength (EX) was 330 nm, and emission wavelength (EM) was 380 nm, scan rate was 2400 nm/min, voltage was 550 V. Each sample was performed in triplicate.

The digestion stable properties of CDs were evaluated through two different digestion procedure according to the method of Bi et al., 2018. The digestion of pure CDs group and digestion of toasted shrimp group. Saliva, gastric juice and small intestinal fluid external digestion model were used to simulate the three stages of human digestive tract.

For the pure CDs groups: according to the above results analysis (parts of 2.3 and 2.4), obtained CDs from toasted shrimp at 230 °C for 40 min (230–40) was used for the digestion experiment. CDs solution (3 mL, 1 × 10−3 mol/L) was mixed with 3 mL simulated saliva fluid (pH 6.0) and stirred at 37 °C for 5 min to mimic oral processing. For the stomach digestion, simulated gastric fluid (7.5 mL, pH 2.0) was added to the above mixture, and then the mixture was stirred with 100 rpm for 2 h at 37 °C. Following, small intestine digestion was mimicked by adding 11.25 mL of intestinal digestion juice, and the mixture was stirred for 2 h at 37 °C. For the each blank group, the same amounts of deionized water was added and adjusted to the same pH. The fluorescence intensity of CDs was measured using fluorescence spectrometer. The EX was 280 nm, all the tests were conducted in triplicate.

For the toasted shrimp groups: the digestion experiments were carried out as above reports. After digestion, CDs were extracted subsequently according to the part of 2.2. The fluorescence intensity of extracted CDs was measured in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Hb interact with CDs

ce:section.ce:para: Effects of Hb (0, 2, 4, 6, 8 and 10 μg/mL) on the fluorescence spectra of CDs (0.1 mg/mL) were examined using fluorescence spectrometer in the dark conditions. The fluorescence spectra was obtained as the emission spectra was 200–600 nm with 280 nm EX, 5 nm slit width, 700 V voltage. During experiments, the inner filter effect (IFE) was also considered and corrected.

Various Hb contents (0–10 μg/mL) on the intrinsic fluorescence of CDs was measured at 298, 304, and 310 K using fluorescence spectrometer, respectively (Liu et al., 2018). CDs concentration was fixed at 0.1 mg/mL. The EX was 280 nm, the EM was 420 nm, scanning voltage was 700 V, the slit width was 5 nm.

The Stern-Volmer equation was used to examine the quenching constants and kind of fluorescence quenching:
(1)
F
0
/F = 1+K
SV [Q] = 1+K
q
τ
0 [Q]
where F0 = the fluorescence intensity of CDs without Hb, F = the fluorescence intensity of CDs with Hb, K
SV = the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, [Q] = the Hb concentration, K
q = the fluorescence quenching rate constant, and τ0 = the average fluorescence lifetime of CDs without quencher.

For static quenching analysis, the binding constant (Ka) and the number of binding points (n) of Hb to CDs could be obtained via the following equation:
(2) log [(F
0
– F)/F] = log K
a + n log[Q]

Where F0 = the fluorescence intensity of CDs without Hb, F = the fluorescence intensity of CDs with Hb, Ka = the binding constant, n = the number of binding points of Hb to CDs could be obtained via the equation.

The Van't Hoff equation was used to analyze the thermodynamic parameters enthalpy change (ΔH°), entropy change (ΔS°), and free energy change (ΔG°) of CDs-Hb complexes (Zhou et al., 2022).
(3) ln K
a = −ΔH/RT + ΔS/R
(4)
ΔG° = ΔH° − TΔS°

Where Ka = the binding constant of Hb with CDs at corresponding temperature, ΔH = the thermodynamic parameters enthalpy change, R = the constant gas (8.314 J mol−1 K−1); T = the various reaction temperature (298, 304, 310 K), ΔG° = the free energy change of CDs-Hb complexes, ΔH° = enthalpy change, ΔS° = entropy change.

The UV–visible spectra of CDs (0.5 mg/mL) -Hb (0.5 mg/mL) mixture were recorded at 298 K by a TU-1810 model spectrophotometer. The circular dichroism (CD) spectra of CDs (0.2–0.5 mg/mL) - Hb (0.5 mg/mL) mixtures were measured using a JASCO Spectrophotometer at 298 K (J-1500 model, Japan Spectroscopic Company, Tokyo, Japan). The measurement methods were similar to Part 2.3.

Data were analyzed by GraphPad Prism 8.4.0 software (San Diego, CA, USA), and then they were described as the mean ± standard deviation (SD). The statistically significant differences were analyzed using one-way ANOVA with Tukey adjustment at the level of p < 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs from the roasted shrimp
ce:section.ce:para: The processing temperature and time on the “blue fluorescence” of various CDs under 365 nm of UV illumination are shown in Fig. 1 . All the samples showed strong “blue fluorescence”, indicating that the existence of CDs in toasted shrimp. And the intensity of “blue fluorescence” was increased with increasing heating time and temperature, which may be due to the increase of fluorophores, which were linked to the surface or interior of the carbon backbone, exhibiting stronger fluorescence emission directly (Yan et al., 2019). Thus, long time of high temperature toasting procedure contributed to form higher contents of CDs in toasted shrimp. Similarity, Song, Wang, Zhang, Yu, and Tan (2018) reported that with the increase of temperature, the intensity of “blue fluorescence” of extracted CDs from toasted chicken increased gradually. TEM image analysis showed that the structure of CDs were irregularly shaped and monodispersed. Based on the TEM images analysis, the average particle sizes of the various CDs (180–40, 200–40, 230–20, 230–30, 230–40) were 97.31 ± 20.47 nm, 93.47 ± 22.97 nm, 79.83 ± 10.07 nm, 80.62 ± 12.77 nm and 58.31 ± 14.15 nm (Fig. S1), respectively. These indicated that long toasting time or high temperature contributed to form smaller size of CDs, which were similar with reports of Li et al. (2017). The proteins, lipids and carbohydrates in shrimp were polymerized into the large nanoparticles at the beginning of toasting, and then it continued to be polymerized and carbonized to form more tightly structural nanoparticles along with the increase of temperature and time.

The influence of toasting temperature and time on the surface chemical characteristics of CDs were determined by FT-IR spectroscopy. Fig. 2 A and B showed that the strong characteristic absorption of all CDs were around 3385 cm−1, which might be due to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H, and 2961 cm−1 were assigned to stretching vibrations of –CH3 and –CH2 (Song, Wu, et al., 2019). Moreover, CDs showed predominance of –COOH groups, aromatic CC or CONH2 groups at 1665 cm−1 and CO groups at 1451 cm−1 (Wang et al., 2017). While, the intensity of 1451 cm−1 slightly decreased with the increase of toasting temperature and time. This might be ascribed to the dissociation of the CO bond of CDs under the higher temperature or longer time of toasting. Besides, the peak shape narrowed at 1665 cm−1, which indicated that the formation of CC in CDs. Moreover, the increase of toasting temperature and time caused the rupture of CO bonds, which was attributed to the fact that CDs underwent further polymerization during the subsequent baking process and became smaller size.

UV–vis absorption spectroscopy (Fig. 2C) showed that all CDs samples had a consistent absorption peaks at 257 nm, which were related to the n-π* electronic transitions, indicating that the presence of CO or C–N functional groups (Li et al., 2017). When the temperature and time reached the highest, the proportion of carbon and nitrogen gradually increased while the proportion of oxygen gradually decreased smaller, and the characteristic absorption peak disappeared because of C–N and/or CO bond breakage. These findings were also consistent with the results of FT-IR spectroscopy.

Chemical shift measurements in 1H NMR spectra can assess the location of electron clouds surrounding hydrogen nuclei. All CDs exhibited a similar 1H NMR signal within the ranges of 0–9.0 ppm. As shown in Fig. 2D, the resonance at 4.697 ppm was due to D2O solvent. The peaks of 0.5–1.5 ppm were due to the 1H of alkyl groups that covalent connected to the surface of the CDs, while peaks of 1.6–2.7 ppm were assigned to the allylic protons adjacent to the sp2 carbon. The protons next to negative-charged atoms were given chemical shifts between 2.4 and 4.5 ppm (Li et al., 2019). These differences were due to the decrease of the proportion of alkyl group in sp3 hybrid structure with the increase of toasting time and temperature, but the increase of the aromatic group of the sp2.

Emission behavior was dependent on the EX, and it was a peculiar phenomenon of CDs (Sk, Jaiswal, Paul, Ghosh, & Chattopadhyay, 2012). As shown in Fig. 3 , with the increase of toasting time and temperature, the maximum EM of CDs was shifted from 320 nm to 340 nm, indicating that the increased sp2 conjugate changed the π electron energy level (Bi et al., 2018). The results were similar to report of Li et al. (2018), which was attributed to their different sizes, the variety of emission centers, as well as the diverse surface states in the suspension (Cong, Bi, Song, Yu, & Tan, 2018).

Different pH, salt contents on the fluorescence intensity (FI) stability of CDs were also investigated. Fig. 4 showed that the initial FI values of all samples were different. In the pH range of 2.0–9.0, CDs showed good FI stability. Higher pH (10.0–11.0) slightly influenced FI of CDs. Normally, NaCl and low concentration of KCl and MgCl2 solutions could not influence the CDs FI, while the higher KCl and MgCl2 contents positively increased the FI. Similarity, Bi et al. (2017) reported that prepared CDs from toasted fish could withstand different concentration of NaCl strengths and showed good resistance to photo-bleaching as pH ranged from 2.0 to 11.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simulated digestion of CDs in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: CDs could enter the digestive system and interacted with functional protein or biological active enzyme in the body (Song, Cao, et al., 2019). To simulate the digestion of CDs in the oral mastication, stomach, and intestine, CDs was digested using simulated saliva, gastric juices, and intestinal digestive fluids in vitro. According to the above results analysis, obtained CDs form toasted shrimps by 230 °C for 40 min (230–40) had the best fluorescence and fluorescence stability. Thus, 230–40 were used for the digestion studies and subsequent experiments. As shown in Fig. S2. A, after 5 min saliva treatment, the FI of CDs was increased by 14.28% ± 0.65%, while stomach digestion quenched the CDs fluorescence about 20.36% ± 0.82%. While, for the following intestinal digestion, the FI of CDs recovered significantly (145.07% ± 2.76%). For the whole shrimp digestion (Fig. S2. B), saliva treatment improved the FI of CDs by 46.54% ± 0.33%, but stomach and intestinal digestion decreased the FI by 50.04% ± 0.37% and 20.58% ± 0.08%, respectively. Thus, in vitro greatly influenced the pure CDs and CDs in the toasted shrimps. This may be related to the changes of pH or the surface functional groups of CDs. All in all, after digestion, CDs still had FI, indicating that it still have high stable structure, and this might make CDs still influence the functional protein or biological active enzyme as it was absorbed in body (Song, Cao, et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of CDs interacted with Hb
ce:section.ce:para: Alterations in the intrinsic fluorescence of CDs could be indicative of changes to their surface functional groups. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the CDs had the highest FI, but with the increase Hb contents, the FI of CDs was gradually decreased. This phenomenon was initiated by the mutual interaction of CDs and Hb, forming ground-state complex that covered the luminescent groups of CDs. Meanwhile, the emission peak of CDs was unchanged, which indicated that any possible interaction could not cause a drastic conformational change of CDs.

Fluorescence quenching mechanisms can be generally divided as static quenching, dynamic quenching and combination of both. The formed complex between the quencher and fluorophore was normally known as static quenching, while collision that occurred throughout the lifetime of an excited state was known as dynamic quenching. According to the value of K q and the change in K sv with rising temperatures. Generally, the low K sv , high K q (>2.0 × 1010 L/mol/s), meaning a static quenching (Zhang et al., 2019). In order to reveal the fluorescence quenching mechanism of Hb on CDs, the Stern-Volmer plots at various temperatures (298 K, 304 K, 310 K) (Fig. 6 ) were obtained. It was a strong linear connection, suggesting it was a single endogenous fluorescence quenching extinction mechanism. Meanwhile, the K sv was steadily decreased with increasing reaction temperatures (Table 1 ), the K q values were ranged from 17.46 to 18.98 × 1012 L/moL/s, which were substantially higher than the maximum diffusion rate constant of the bio-molecule (2.0 × 1010 L/mol/s), indicating a static quenching (Liu et al., 2019). Thus, the formed complex between CDs and Hb decreased the intrinsic fluorescence spectra of CDs.

The K a and n values of Hb with CDs at three different temperatures were calculated using the relationship between [Q] and F
0 /F and Equation (2). As listed in Table 1, the n of three temperature were detected to be about 2 or 3, indicating a good binding affinity to Hb of CDs under physiological conditions with two or three molecules of Hb bound with CDs. The higher temperature, the less number of Hb was bound. The K a was decreased with the rise of reaction temperatures, showing that the binding ability was slightly decreased (Wang et al., 2019).

The primary driving factors involved in the creation of the complexes can be identified using ΔH°, ΔG° and ΔS° between the interaction of CDs and Hb at various temperatures. Hydrogen bonding (ΔH° < 0 and ΔS° < 0), van der Waals force (ΔH° < 0 and ΔS° < 0), electrostatic interactions (ΔH° < 0, ΔS° > 0) and hydrophobic interactions (ΔH° > 0 and ΔS° > 0) were the four primary forms of interactions between small molecule and bio-macromolecule (Wang et al., 2015). Herein, Hb showed that spontaneous interaction with CDs, which was supported by a negative in Gibbs free energy. The ΔH° and ΔS° were both negative, revealing that hydrogen bonding interactions and van der Waals force were the predominant driving force for the binding of CDs and Hb. Thus, thermodynamic parameters result also supported that CDs could interact with Hb to form stable CDs-Hb complex.

UV–vis absorption measurement could be used to explore protein-ligand complex through the investigation of protein structural variations (Cao et al., 2018). As depicted in Fig. 5B, Hb had the two absorption peaks, the weak absorption of Hb at 277 nm corresponded to the π-π* transition of the aromatic amino acids (Trp, Tyr and Phe) (Liu et al., 2019). The UV absorption of CDs-Hb at 277 nm was increased with the addition of Hb, showing a gradual blue-shift of 19 nm. Another peak at 405 nm of Hb was belong to the porphyrin Soret band (Wang et al., 2015) and the UV absorption at 405 nm was decreased with the addition of CDs. These results suggested that the CDs interacted with Hb to loosen and unfold the protein skeleton (Chi et al., 2016). It had been reported that dynamic quenching changes fluorescence lifetime of protein, while static quenching altered UV–vis spectrum (Cao et al., 2018). Thus, herein, the UV results further supported that the fluorescence quenching type of Hb was primarily static quenching.

CD spectroscopy could be used to investigate CDs on the secondary structure of Hb. Fig. 5C expressed that the mean residue ellipticity (MRE) of Hb/Hb-CDs showed two minima at 209 (π-π*) and 222 nm (n-π*), which were due to the α-helix and random coil of Hb, respectively (Cao et al., 2018). When CDs was added, the CD spectral profile of Hb underwent an increase in CD signal without a discernible shift in the peak wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 5D, the content of α-helix was dropped from 50.8% to 45.2% with the increase of CDs concentration. These demonstrated that CDs caused the native Hb disorder. The loosening and unfolding of Hb skeleton caused by the binding of CDs may have an impact on the protein biological function by destabilizing the polypeptide chain. In conclusion, CDs could be attached to the amino acid residues of Hb chains and wrecked hydrogen bonds, altering Hb structure (Song, Cao, Cong, Song, & Tan, 2018).

A thorough investigation of the temperature and time on the structural and biological characteristics of CDs from the toasted shrimps had been carried out. Changes of toasting temperature and time were accompanied by the changes of particle size, fluorescence properties and chemical bonds of CDs. Specifically, high temperature made CDs smaller size and better fluorescence luminescence characteristics than those were formed at low temperature. The higher pH, KCl and MgCl2 contents changed CDs structure by alteration of the fluorescence stability. In vitro result indicated that CDs still had high structural stability after digestion. CDs obviously interacted with Hb by the hydrogen bonding interactions and van der Waals force, showing a static quenching behavior. Moreover, CDs caused Hb structural alteration by loosening and unfolding of Hb skeleton, influenced the biological activity of Hb. All these findings will help consumers better understand the Barbecue industry through another perspective. However, there still needs lots of works to demonstrate the influence of CDs on the human health through animal experiment as well as evaluate the accurate chemical structures and compositions of the CDs.

Tao Huang: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Shiyi Peng: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zongcai Tu: Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis. Yue Chao: Methodology, Investigation. Shengnan Zhan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Young Science and Technology Innovation Leading Talents of Ningbo (2023QL038), Young PHD Innovation Research Plan of Ningbo (2023J380), National Key R&D Program of China (2020YFD0900903), the Open Project Program of National R&D Center for Freshwater Fish Processing, Jiangxi Normal University (NC2FP-KF-202301).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fbio.2024.103814.
